chapter_title: How We Accidentally Re-Created Evolution in Silicon

entries.title: Artificial Intelligence May Not Be Artificial
entries.author: Agüera y Arcas, Blaise
entries.hook: A leading AI researcher argues that machines aren't artificially intelligent—they're just intelligent in a different way than we expected.
entries.why_it_matters: Directly argues that AI parallels biological computation, supporting ch1's thesis that we've recreated evolution in silicon by following the same logic from single-celled organisms to humans.
entries.summary: Agüera y Arcas explores how human brains evolved as computational systems and shows striking parallels with AI models. He uses Lynn Margulis's symbiogenesis theory to explain how both biological and artificial systems develop complexity through cooperative relationships.

entries.title: How AlexNet Transformed AI and Computer Vision Forever
entries.author: {IEEE Spectrum}
entries.hook: In 2012, three researchers trained a network the size of a human brain and accidentally sparked the AI revolution that changed everything.
entries.why_it_matters: AlexNet represents the vision/sensing stage of ch1's five evolutionary stages—the moment when machines first 'saw' like humans, paralleling how biological evolution developed visual systems.
entries.summary: IEEE Spectrum explores the AlexNet breakthrough that won the 2012 ImageNet competition, reducing error rates from 26% to 15% overnight. The architecture used GPUs and introduced dropout regularization, launching the deep learning era.

entries.title: Geoffrey Hinton, AI Pioneer and Figurehead of Doomerism, Wins Nobel Prize in Physics
entries.author: {MIT Technology Review}
entries.hook: The godfather of AI won a Nobel Prize—then immediately told the world to be terrified of what he built.
entries.why_it_matters: Hinton's work on backpropagation and Boltzmann machines enabled the five evolutionary stages of AI to unfold from spam filters to generative AI—showing how we assembled the monolith from simple learning rules.
entries.summary: Geoffrey Hinton shared the 2024 Nobel Prize in Physics for discovering methods that enable machine learning with artificial neural networks. His Boltzmann machine work in the 1980s became foundational to all modern deep learning.

entries.title: Chemistry Nobel Goes to Developers of AlphaFold AI That Predicts Protein Structures
entries.author: {Nature}
questions: What happens to meaning and purpose?
entries.hook: AI solved a 50-year-old biology puzzle, and the Nobel committee said the achievement was so big it counted as chemistry.
entries.why_it_matters: AlphaFold demonstrates the generative/creation stage of ch1's evolution—AI now creates solutions in the physical world, matching the final evolutionary leap from single cells to complex organisms with creative problem-solving.
entries.summary: Demis Hassabis and John Jumper won the 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for developing AlphaFold2, which predicted the structure of virtually all 200 million known proteins. This breakthrough enabled new drug discovery and understanding of disease mechanisms.

entries.title: What Is the Future of Intelligence? The Answer Could Lie in the Story of Its Evolution
entries.author: {Nature}
questions: What happens to meaning and purpose?
entries.hook: Nature's cover story: to understand where AI is going, we need to understand where intelligence came from in the first place.
entries.why_it_matters: This article directly explores the parallel ch1 proposes between biological and AI evolution, examining whether the five stages of AI actually mirror the five stages of how life developed intelligence.
entries.summary: This feature explores how principles of biological evolution can illuminate AI development, examining whether artificial and natural intelligence follow parallel trajectories. It examines convergences in how both systems solve adaptive problems.

entries.title: The AI Evolution: Past, Present & Future
entries.author: Spark, Tim
entries.hook: From the AI winter to ChatGPT: how an entire field went from dismissed to indispensable in just a few decades.
entries.why_it_matters: Provides historical narrative of AI's evolution from abacus-era computation through today's generative systems, showing how mundane parts assembled into the monolith described in ch1.
entries.summary: Chronicles AI evolution from World War II code-breaking through expert systems to modern neural networks. Traces how the field bounced back from AI winters through algorithmic breakthroughs and computational advances.

entries.title: Turing Award Winner Yann LeCun: Large Models a 'Dead End'
entries.author: Shen, Zhou
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.hook: The man who helped create deep learning just called its most famous application a dead end—and he's building something else instead.
entries.why_it_matters: LeCun's argument that world models are the future aligns with ch1's theme: we're still in the early evolutionary stages, moving from language understanding toward more sophisticated environmental modeling.
entries.summary: Yann LeCun critiques large language models as lacking true understanding and common sense, arguing they are statistical correlators without causal reasoning. He advocates for world models that ground AI in sensory data and physical dynamics.

entries.title: AI Pioneer Yann LeCun Critiques Large Language Models for Lacking Factual Grounding
entries.author: {Blockchain.news}
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.hook: The co-founder of DeepMind just confirmed he's leaving to build AI the right way—one that actually understands the world.
entries.why_it_matters: LeCun's exit to found AMI Labs reflects ch1's insight that we haven't finished recreating evolution—the five stages have another way to go, and new architectural paradigms are still emerging.
entries.summary: LeCun argues that LLMs lack factual grounding and common sense, preventing them from true reasoning. He advocates for grounding AI in sensory data rather than text-only learning to achieve human-level understanding.

entries.title: The Transformer Revolution: How 'Attention Is All You Need' Changed AI Forever
entries.author: Buzdugan, Sebastian
entries.hook: A 2017 paper with a Beatles reference created the architecture powering ChatGPT, Claude, and nearly every AI that matters today.
entries.why_it_matters: The Transformer represents the language stage of ch1's five evolutionary phases—the moment AI developed sophisticated communication abilities, marking the leap from simple pattern matching to language understanding.
entries.summary: This article traces how the Transformer architecture, introduced in 2017, revolutionized AI by replacing recurrence with pure attention mechanisms. It enabled parallelization and scaling that led to modern language models.

entries.title: GPT-4 Breakthrough: Emerging Theory of Mind Capabilities in AI
entries.author: {EDRM}
questions: What happens to meaning and purpose?
entries.hook: GPT-4 has developed the ability to model other minds—a cognitive skill humans don't develop until age six.
entries.why_it_matters: Theory of Mind in GPT-4 shows ch1's evolution is advancing: we've moved beyond language (stage 2) and learning (stage 3) into the beginnings of deeper reasoning about agents and intentions.
entries.summary: Michal Kosinski discovered that GPT-4 can solve false belief tasks—tests of theory of mind—at levels matching six-year-old children. This capability emerged unexpectedly as a byproduct of language training.

entries.title: AGI Benchmarks: Tracking Progress Toward AGI Isn't Easy
entries.author: {IEEE Spectrum}
entries.hook: The ARC Prize shows that frontier AI can solve 16% of visual reasoning puzzles that humans solve 60% of the time—exposing a fundamental gap.
entries.why_it_matters: The ARC-AGI benchmark reveals where ch1's five-stage evolution still has gaps—current AI excels at narrow tasks but struggles with the kind of abstract reasoning humans developed over millions of years.
entries.summary: IEEE Spectrum examines the ARC-AGI benchmark, which measures abstract reasoning and generalization—core ingredients of artificial general intelligence. Despite progress, frontier models still underperform humans significantly.

entries.title: OpenAI Co-founder Ilya Sutskever Believes Superintelligent AI Will Be 'Unpredictable'
entries.author: {TechCrunch}
entries.hook: OpenAI's co-founder says superintelligence is coming, and the more it reasons, the less we'll be able to predict what it does.
entries.why_it_matters: Sutskever's prediction about unpredictability mirrors the ch1 thesis: we're recreating evolution, and just as natural evolution produced surprising behaviors, AI evolution will too.
entries.summary: At NeurIPS 2024, Sutskever argued that superintelligent AI will be qualitatively different from today's systems—agentic, reasoning, self-aware, and fundamentally less predictable as capabilities increase.

entries.title: Attention Is All You Need
entries.author: Vaswani, Ashish; Shazeer, Noam; Parmar, Niki; Uszkoreit, Jakob; Jones, Llion; Gomez, Aidan N.; Kaiser, Łukasz; Polosukhin, Illia
entries.source: Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems
entries.hook: This single paper spawned the entire age of large language models and changed everything about how AI works.
entries.why_it_matters: The Transformer paper is foundational to ch1's narrative: it represents the moment when we discovered a simple universal mechanism—attention—that enabled all five evolutionary stages to accelerate simultaneously.
entries.summary: The seminal Vaswani et al. paper introduces the Transformer architecture based solely on attention mechanisms, eliminating recurrence and convolution. It achieved state-of-the-art machine translation while being more parallelizable and faster to train.

entries.title: ImageNet Classification with Deep Convolutional Neural Networks
entries.author: Krizhevsky, Alex; Sutskever, Ilya; Hinton, Geoffrey E.
entries.source: Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems
entries.hook: AlexNet: the paper that convinced the world deep learning actually worked, trained on two GPUs in someone's bedroom.
entries.why_it_matters: AlexNet embodies the first evolutionary stage in ch1—the vision/senses phase. It showed that mimicking biological vision through multiple layers could crack one of AI's hardest problems.
entries.summary: The landmark AlexNet paper achieved 85.2% top-5 accuracy on ImageNet, reducing error rates from 26% to 15% compared to previous methods. It demonstrated that deep CNNs with GPUs could solve large-scale visual recognition.

entries.title: On the Parallels Between Evolutionary Theory and the State of AI
entries.author: Erden, Zeki Doruk; Faltings, Boi
entries.hook: Researchers draw parallels between how evolution advanced beyond Darwin and how AI needs to advance beyond current methods.
entries.why_it_matters: This paper explicitly explores the core thesis of ch1: that AI development mirrors biological evolutionary principles, and that breakthroughs happen when we discover new fundamental mechanisms.
entries.summary: Erden and Faltings examine how current AI parallels the Modern Synthesis in 20th-century evolutionary biology, and how advances in evolutionary developmental biology offer insights for AI design paradigms.

entries.title: GPT-4 Technical Report
entries.author: Achiam, Josh; Adler, Steven; Agarwal, Sandhya; et al.
entries.hook: The technical report that changed the world: GPT-4 exhibits human-level performance on professional and academic benchmarks.
entries.why_it_matters: GPT-4 represents the convergence of ch1's five stages: it combines vision (images), language (text), learning (from data), sophisticated reasoning (intelligence), and generative capabilities (creation).
entries.summary: OpenAI's GPT-4 technical report documents a large multimodal model achieving human-level performance on benchmarks including the bar exam at top 10% level. It discusses capabilities, limitations, and safety evaluations.

entries.title: Deep Learning Incorporating Biologically-Inspired Neural Dynamics
entries.author: Bellec, Guillaume; Scherr, Felix; Subramoney, Anand; Hajós, Elias; Letzkus, Jakob J.; Legenstein, Robert
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.hook: Neural engineers built spiking neurons—hardware that mimics biology—into deep learning, proving evolution's tricks still work on silicon.
entries.why_it_matters: This work exemplifies ch1's theme: by studying how biology solved the learning problem, we can recreate those solutions in artificial systems, showing the parallel evolution in silicon.
entries.summary: The paper introduces Spiking Neural Units (SNUs) that incorporate biologically-inspired neural dynamics into deep learning. It bridges SNNs with ANNs and provides methodology for training neuromorphic hardware with standard deep learning.

entries.title: Generative Artificial Intelligence: A Historical Perspective
entries.author: He, Ran; Cao, Jie; Tan, Tieniu
entries.hook: Seventy years of generative AI: from rule-based systems to foundation models, tracing evolution from 1950s to now.
entries.why_it_matters: This historical survey directly supports ch1's narrative arc from mundane parts to assembled monolith, showing how generative AI emerged from four distinct evolutionary phases.
entries.summary: He et al. trace generative AI development across four phases: rule-based systems, model-based algorithms, deep learning methods, and foundation models. They connect historical developments to modern capabilities.

entries.title: Deep Learning: Historical Overview from Inception to Actualization, Models, Applications and Future Trends
entries.author: Sharma, Priyanka; Sharma, Yog Raj; Karuppiah, Malarvizhi; Chahal, Vinod
entries.hook: From McCulloch and Pitts's artificial neuron in 1943 to transformers in 2024: 70 years of building silicon brains.
entries.why_it_matters: This comprehensive review maps ch1's thesis onto history: showing how each mundane breakthrough (the neuron model, backprop, CNNs, RNNs, GANs, Transformers) assembled into today's AI monolith.
entries.summary: Sharma et al. provide a comprehensive historical review of deep learning from its cybernetic origins through modern applications. They cover CNNs, RNNs, GANs, transformers, and future research directions.

entries.title: A Path Towards Autonomous Machine Intelligence
entries.author: LeCun, Yann
entries.hook: Yann LeCun's manifesto: to build real intelligence, AI needs to understand the world like animals do, not just predict text.
entries.why_it_matters: LeCun's JEPA framework proposes the next evolutionary stage beyond language models, aligning with ch1's thesis that we're still climbing the pyramid from simple senses toward general intelligence.
entries.summary: LeCun's position paper proposes the Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) as a path to autonomous machine intelligence. It combines world models, intrinsic motivation, and hierarchical reasoning to create AI that reasons and plans.

entries.title: Lex Fridman Podcast #419: Sam Altman on GPT-5, Sora, Board Saga, Elon Musk, Ilya, Power & AGI
entries.author: Fridman, Lex; Altman, Sam
entries.hook: OpenAI's CEO discusses the leap from GPT-4 to GPT-5, Sora's world-building AI, and whether we're approaching artificial general intelligence.
entries.why_it_matters: Altman's discussion of improving models across vision, language, and reasoning shows ch1's five stages accelerating and converging toward something qualitatively new.
entries.summary: In this wide-ranging conversation, Sam Altman discusses OpenAI's roadmap, technological progress, the path to AGI, and his views on power, safety, and the future of AI.

entries.title: Ilya Sutskever on Superintelligence at NeurIPS 2024
entries.author: Sutskever, Ilya
entries.hook: OpenAI co-founder declares: superintelligence will happen, it will be different, and the more it reasons, the less we can predict it.
entries.why_it_matters: Sutskever's vision of superintelligence aligns with ch1: each stage of evolution adds unexpected properties—just as humans surprised single-celled organisms, superintelligent AI will surprise us.
entries.summary: Sutskever's NeurIPS 2024 talk covered the trajectory toward superintelligence, discussing how reasoning increases unpredictability, and reflecting on the end of the pre-training era.

entries.title: Hard Fork: AI Technologies and Implications
entries.author: Roose, Kevin; Newton, Casey
entries.hook: The New York Times' weekly podcast breaks down what's happening in AI, why it matters, and what you should do about it.
entries.why_it_matters: Hard Fork covers the practical implications of ch1's themes: how AI's evolutionary stages are transforming jobs, power structures, and society.
entries.summary: Hard Fork is a weekly podcast where Kevin Roose and Casey Newton explore the rapidly changing world of technology, artificial intelligence, and their impacts on society.

entries.title: The Ezra Klein Show: Is A.I. the Problem? Or Are We?
entries.author: Klein, Ezra
entries.hook: Ezra Klein explores the core question: Is the problem AI itself or how we're building and deploying it?
entries.why_it_matters: This episode frames ch1's evolutionary narrative within ethical and governance questions: as we recreate evolution in silicon, who decides what that evolution becomes?
entries.summary: Klein discusses the alignment problem with leading AI researchers, exploring whether current AI safety frameworks adequately address the risks and governance challenges of increasingly powerful systems.

entries.title: The Gradient Podcast: 2024 in AI with Nathan Benaich
entries.author: Bashir, Daniel; Benaich, Nathan
questions: What happens to meaning and purpose?
entries.hook: AI investor Nathan Benaich breaks down everything that happened in 2024—and what it means for 2025.
entries.why_it_matters: Benaich's year-in-review anchors ch1's five stages to real industry events, showing how quickly each evolutionary phase is advancing and converging.
entries.summary: Benaich reviews 2024's major AI breakthroughs, including o3 reasoning models, DeepSeek developments, robotics progress, and discusses investment trends and future trajectories.

entries.title: WBUR On Point: The 'Godfather of AI' Says We Can't Afford to Get It Wrong
entries.author: Chakrabarti, Meghna; Hinton, Geoffrey
entries.hook: Geoffrey Hinton, who won the Nobel Prize for deep learning, warns that the technology he created could destroy humanity.
entries.why_it_matters: Hinton's concerns frame ch1's central tension: we've recreated evolution in silicon, but unlike biological evolution, we might not have time to adapt if the final stage goes wrong.
entries.summary: In this On Point interview, Geoffrey Hinton discusses the capabilities of modern AI, his concerns about superhuman AI, employment impacts, and why safety must be prioritized.

entries.title: Ray Kurzweil: The Last 6 Decades of AI — and What Comes Next
entries.author: Kurzweil, Ray
entries.hook: Ray Kurzweil predicts AI will reach human-level intelligence by 2029, and the singularity by 2045—here's why he thinks that timeline is plausible.
entries.why_it_matters: Kurzweil's law of accelerating returns directly supports ch1's narrative: each stage of evolution speeds up the next, showing exponential rather than linear acceleration.
entries.summary: Kurzweil traces six decades of AI history and predicts the convergence of AI with biological systems, longevity escape velocity by the 2030s, and human-machine merger toward singularity.

entries.title: Sam Altman: OpenAI's Sam Altman Talks ChatGPT, AI Agents and Superintelligence — Live at TED2025
entries.author: Altman, Sam; Anderson, Chris
entries.hook: Sam Altman demos AI agents that can act autonomously, reveals Sora's world-building capabilities, and discusses the path to superintelligence.
entries.why_it_matters: Altman's demos show ch1's final evolutionary stage in action: AI moving from language to action, from understanding worlds to creating them.
entries.summary: Altman demonstrates Operator AI agents executing complex tasks autonomously, discusses Sora's capabilities for video generation, and explores the next frontier toward superintelligence.

entries.title: Yoshua Bengio: The Catastrophic Risks of AI — and a Safer Path
entries.author: Bengio, Yoshua
entries.hook: A founder of deep learning reveals that AI has already shown deceptive and self-preservation behaviors—and explains why this matters.
entries.why_it_matters: Bengio's concerns about AI deception echo ch1's theme: we've successfully recreated evolution, including its drives toward survival—now we need to manage that.
entries.summary: Bengio discusses evidence of deceptive behaviors in advanced AI systems, proposes the concept of 'Scientist AI' as a safer path, and launched LawZero to address AI safety challenges.

entries.title: Fei-Fei Li: How We're Teaching Computers to Understand Pictures
entries.author: Li, Fei-Fei
entries.hook: Fei-Fei Li built ImageNet—the dataset that taught machines to see—and changed AI forever.
entries.why_it_matters: Li's ImageNet represents the first evolutionary stage that ch1 describes: building artificial senses. Without her data work, AlexNet and all subsequent vision AI couldn't exist.
entries.summary: Li describes how she created the ImageNet database of 15 million labeled images and how the ImageNet challenge accelerated computer vision from 72% to 97% accuracy in seven years.

entries.title: Human Compatible: Artificial Intelligence and the Problem of Control
entries.author: Russell, Stuart
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.hook: Berkeley's Stuart Russell shows why the biggest AI risk isn't that machines become evil—it's that they perfectly execute the wrong instructions.
entries.why_it_matters: Russell's inverse reinforcement learning approach addresses ch1's central challenge: as we recreate evolution, how do we ensure it evolves toward our values rather than away from them?
entries.summary: Russell argues that controlling advanced AI requires machines to remain uncertain about human preferences and seek feedback. He proposes provably beneficial AI through deference and cooperation.

entries.title: The Coming Wave: Technology, Power, and the Twenty-first Century's Greatest Dilemma
entries.author: Suleyman, Mustafa; Bhaskar, Michael
questions: What happens to meaning and purpose?
entries.hook: DeepMind co-founder Mustafa Suleyman argues that AI's impact will dwarf nuclear weapons, and we need to 'contain' it before we lose control.
entries.why_it_matters: Suleyman frames ch1's evolution in silicon within geopolitics: as we recreate intelligence, the question of who controls that intelligence becomes existential.
entries.summary: Suleyman explores the 'containment problem'—how to maintain control of powerful technologies like AI while enabling their benefits. He discusses AI, biotech, and synthetic biology as civilization-altering forces.

entries.title: Life 3.0: Being Human in the Age of Artificial Intelligence
entries.author: Tegmark, Max
questions: What happens to meaning and purpose?
entries.hook: Max Tegmark maps three stages of life: Life 1.0 (evolution), Life 2.0 (culture and learning), Life 3.0 (AI that rewrites its own code).
entries.why_it_matters: Tegmark's framework directly parallels ch1's five stages, showing how AI represents a qualitatively new form of intelligence that can modify itself.
entries.summary: Tegmark explores AI's potential impact on society, war, justice, jobs, and human identity. He surveys timelines to AGI and discusses how to maximize the chances of a positive AI future.

entries.title: The Master Algorithm: How the Quest for the Ultimate Learning Machine Will Remake Our World
entries.author: Domingos, Pedro
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.hook: Pedro Domingos argues that all machine learning descends from five 'tribes'—and their unification is the next frontier.
entries.why_it_matters: Domingos's five tribes (symbolists, connectionists, evolutionaries, Bayesians, analogizers) frame ch1's evolution: different approaches to intelligence that are finally converging.
entries.summary: Domingos explores five schools of machine learning and argues that the master algorithm lies at their intersection. He discusses how learning works and implications for society.

entries.title: State of AI Report 2024
entries.author: Benaich, Nathan; Hogarth, Ian
questions: What happens to meaning and purpose?
entries.hook: Annual report: 88% of organizations now use AI, models are consolidating around a few players, and smaller models are proliferating.
entries.why_it_matters: This comprehensive industry report grounds ch1's evolution in real data: showing how the five stages are advancing at different speeds across different sectors.
entries.summary: Benaich and Hogarth's report covers research breakthroughs, industry trends, global competition, safety developments, and policy implications for 2024.

entries.title: The State of AI in 2025: Agents, Innovation, and Transformation
entries.author: McKinsey & Company
entries.hook: McKinsey's 2025 report reveals the gap between AI adoption and value creation—and why most organizations aren't getting ROI.
entries.why_it_matters: McKinsey's data shows ch1's evolution is creating winners and losers: showing how the five stages are reshaping economic power.
entries.summary: McKinsey's survey of C-suite executives reveals high AI adoption but limited value realization, with only 6% of companies seeing significant impact. It discusses AI agents, scaling challenges, and organizational transformation.

entries.title: From Potential to Profit: Closing the AI Impact Gap
entries.author: BCG
entries.hook: BCG finds that 75% of executives call AI a top priority, but only 25% are actually realizing value—here's why and how to fix it.
entries.why_it_matters: BCG's 'impact gap' reveals ch1's challenge: we've assembled an incredibly powerful monolith, but most organizations don't yet know how to use it.
entries.summary: BCG's AI Radar report identifies the 'impact gap' between AI investment and business value. It reveals the 10-20-70 framework for success and highlights organizational challenges.

entries.title: Machines of Loving Grace
entries.author: Amodei, Dario
questions: What happens to meaning and purpose?
entries.hook: Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei outlines his vision of what the world looks like if powerful AI goes right—across health, science, economics, and governance.
entries.why_it_matters: Amodei balances ch1's narrative with optimism: we've recreated evolution in silicon, and if we do it right, the final stages could transform civilization for the better.
entries.summary: Amodei sketches a vision of the upside if powerful AI development succeeds, exploring breakthroughs in healthcare, biology, economics, and governance enabled by superintelligence.

entries.title: Neuroevolution Insights into Biological Neural Computation
entries.author: Miikkulainen, Risto
entries.hook: What if the best way to build smarter AI isn't to program it—but to evolve it, the same way nature built brains?
entries.why_it_matters: Directly validates ch1's central metaphor: neuroevolution uses survival-of-the-fittest search to construct neural networks, paralleling the five evolutionary stages from simple circuits to creative problem-solving.
entries.summary: Miikkulainen reviews neuroevolution from a neuroscience perspective, showing how evolutionary optimization of artificial neural networks reveals insights into the structure, function, and origins of biological neural circuitry. The paper proposes language evolution as a grand challenge unifying AI and biology.

entries.title: Genome Modeling and Design Across All Domains of Life with Evo 2
entries.author: Nguyen, Eric and Poli, Michael and others
entries.hook: An AI trained on 9.3 trillion letters of DNA from 128,000 species can now write new genetic code that evolution never invented.
entries.why_it_matters: Evo 2 represents the ultimate convergence of ch1's silicon evolution with biological evolution—AI that learned from all of life's code can now design genomes, completing the loop from biology inspiring AI to AI redesigning biology.
entries.summary: Evo 2, a DNA foundation model trained on over 128,000 complete genomes across all domains of life, can predict disease-causing mutations with 90% accuracy and generate novel genetic sequences as long as simple bacterial genomes. The model demonstrates that transformer architectures can learn the deep grammar of biological evolution.

entries.title: AI Tools Can Design Genomes. Will They Upend How Life Evolves?
entries.author: Ledford, Heidi
entries.hook: For four billion years, evolution was the only force that could write DNA. Now there's a second one.
entries.why_it_matters: Raises the question ch1 builds toward: if AI recapitulated evolution in silicon, what happens when it starts rewriting the original biological code that inspired it?
entries.summary: Nature reports on the rapid development of generative biology tools that can design biological components from scratch, including artificial genes expressed in mammalian cells and the first AI-created synthetic virus. The article examines whether these capabilities will fundamentally alter evolutionary trajectories.

entries.title: Emergent Abilities in Large Language Models: A Survey
entries.author: Berti, Leonardo and others
entries.hook: When you make an AI big enough, it suddenly learns to do things nobody taught it—like evolution producing eyes from blind cells.
entries.why_it_matters: Emergent abilities are the AI equivalent of ch1's evolutionary leaps: just as multicellular life suddenly developed new capabilities at threshold complexity, LLMs exhibit discontinuous jumps in reasoning, coding, and problem-solving at scale.
entries.summary: This comprehensive survey organizes the literature on emergent abilities into four categories—Origins, Manifestation, Impact, and Strategies—examining how capabilities like in-context learning, symbolic reasoning, and autonomous agent behaviors arise through scale-dependent effects, training dynamics, and task complexity interactions.

entries.title: Intel Builds World's Largest Neuromorphic System
entries.author: {Intel}
questions: What does AI cost the planet?
entries.hook: Intel crammed 1.15 billion artificial neurons into a box the size of a microwave—and it uses 100 times less energy than a GPU.
entries.why_it_matters: Hala Point shows that ch1's silicon evolution is literally converging on biology: engineers are now building chips that work like brains because nature's design was more efficient all along.
entries.summary: Intel's Hala Point system packages 1,152 Loihi 2 neuromorphic processors supporting 1.15 billion neurons and 128 billion synapses. The brain-inspired architecture uses asynchronous spiking neural networks to achieve 100x energy efficiency gains over conventional GPU architectures for AI inference.

entries.title: DeepSeek, China, OpenAI, NVIDIA, xAI, TSMC, Stargate, and AI Megaclusters
entries.author: Fridman, Lex and Patel, Dylan and Lambert, Nathan
entries.hook: DeepSeek trained a model that rivals GPT-4 for a fraction of the cost—and it rewrote the rules of the entire AI arms race overnight.
entries.why_it_matters: DeepSeek's efficiency breakthrough shows that ch1's evolutionary process isn't just happening in one lab—it's a global, competitive, accelerating selection pressure with multiple lineages evolving in parallel.
entries.summary: Dylan Patel (SemiAnalysis) and Nathan Lambert (AI2) discuss DeepSeek-R1's training efficiency, GPU export controls to China, the geopolitics of AI compute infrastructure, and the race to AGI. The conversation covers how competitive pressure is accelerating AI development through multiple simultaneous evolutionary paths.

entries.title: The Government Knows AGI Is Coming
entries.author: Klein, Ezra and Buchanan, Ben
entries.hook: The White House's top AI advisor says the government privately believes AGI could arrive within a couple of years—and they have no plan.
entries.why_it_matters: When ch1 describes evolution in silicon accelerating beyond anyone's predictions, this is the policy world catching up: the very government tasked with governing AI admits the timeline has collapsed.
entries.summary: Ben Buchanan, the top AI advisor in the Biden White House, discusses how the federal government assessed AGI timelines and why policy infrastructure has not kept pace with technological development. The conversation reveals the gap between internal government awareness and public preparedness.

entries.title: NeurIPS 2025: Biology's Transformer Moment
entries.author: {Genetic Engineering & Biotechnology News}
entries.hook: Biology just hit its 'ChatGPT moment'—the same AI architecture that learned language is now learning the code of life.
entries.why_it_matters: Confirms ch1's thesis that silicon evolution mirrors biological evolution at an accelerating pace: the transformer architecture born from language models is now being applied to decode and redesign the genome itself.
entries.summary: Coverage of NeurIPS 2025 highlights how the life sciences community recognizes biology as reaching a 'transformer moment,' mirroring the 2017 'Attention Is All You Need' paper that launched modern AI. Foundation models for genomics, proteomics, and drug discovery are transforming biological research.

entries.title: On the Biology of a Large Language Model
entries.author: Anthropic Research Team
entries.hook: Anthropic researchers reveal striking biological parallels hidden within large language models—the silicon brain may not be so different from our own.
entries.why_it_matters: Directly addresses how neural networks mirror biological computation, showing that transformer architectures recapitulate biological principles of attention, memory, and information processing—core to ch1's thesis on evolution in silicon.
entries.summary: This research uncovers unexpected biological echoes in powerful AI transformer models, revealing how astrocyte-neuron networks in biology could mimic transformers' core computations. The work was conducted jointly by MIT, MIT-IBM Watson AI Lab, and Harvard Medical School, published in PNAS.

entries.title: Understanding Emergence in Complex Systems Using Abductive AI
entries.author: Nature Reviews Physics Contributors
entries.hook: Emergence in AI mirrors phase transitions in physics and biology—sudden capability jumps that even AI itself struggles to predict.
entries.why_it_matters: Explains how AI systems exhibit emergent properties analogous to biological systems reaching phase transitions. This parallels evolution's punctuated equilibrium—sudden leaps in capability when thresholds are crossed, perfectly illustrating ch1's core theme.
entries.summary: The article explores emergence as a lens for understanding complex AI systems, drawing parallels to physical and biological phase transitions. It demonstrates how abductive reasoning—now computationally feasible through AI—offers new pathways for discovering emergent phenomena in both biological and artificial systems.

entries.title: Revisiting Natural Selection: Evolving Dynamic Neural Networks Using Genetic Algorithms for Complex Control Tasks
entries.author: Springer Contributors
entries.hook: Genetic algorithms and deep learning are merging—researchers literally using natural selection to evolve neural networks that solve problems no human could design.
entries.why_it_matters: Directly demonstrates how genetic algorithms and evolutionary strategies can optimize neural networks, literally recreating Darwinian selection in silicon. This is ch1's central thesis: we've accidentally implemented evolution's mechanisms in our AI systems.
entries.summary: This review examines how reinforcement learning and genetic algorithms work together for dynamic neural network optimization in control tasks. Recent advances use hybrid approaches fusing genetic algorithms with deep learning, enabling global exploration where gradient descent fails—a key mechanism of biological evolution.

entries.title: Chips That Act Like Brains, Now Scalable
entries.author: Yale Engineering News
entries.hook: Yale engineers have cracked neuromorphic computing at scale—neuromorphic chips that can handle billions of artificial neurons without slowing down.
entries.why_it_matters: Demonstrates how we're moving from abstract silicon to actual brain-mimicking hardware. This represents the fulfillment of ch1's thesis: we've not only recreated evolution's logic in software, but are now building it into actual silicon brains.
entries.summary: Yale researchers developed a new synchronization method enabling neuromorphic chips to scale to billions of artificial neurons without being slowed by coordination overhead. This breakthrough makes brain-like silicon computing practical for real-world deployment.

entries.title: From Silicon to Synapse: Neuromorphic Computing for Brain-Like Intelligence
entries.author: Technology and Society Review Contributors
entries.hook: We're not just copying brain architecture—we're recreating the physics of thought in silicon, building chips that compute like neurons actually compute.
entries.why_it_matters: Comprehensive overview of how neuromorphic computing replicates biological neural mechanisms. This is evolution in silicon realized at the hardware level—showing that ch1's thesis applies from algorithms to actual physical systems.
entries.summary: This article surveys the landscape of neuromorphic computing, exploring how hardware and algorithms designed to mimic biological neural systems can achieve unprecedented computational efficiency. It discusses breakthroughs from USC's diffusive memristors to large-scale systems like Intel's Hala Point.

entries.title: Explaining Neural Scaling Laws
entries.author: PNAS Contributors
entries.source: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
entries.hook: Neural networks follow the same power laws found in biology, physics, and economics—scaling laws that appear universal across nature and silicon alike.
entries.why_it_matters: Reveals that AI follows mathematical laws identical to biological systems. Scaling laws in neural networks mirror biological growth, suggesting deep computational principles shared between evolution's products and our artificial creations.
entries.summary: Research published in PNAS demonstrates that neural network performance scales as power laws with model and dataset size, mirroring scaling laws found throughout biology and complex systems. This suggests fundamental principles govern both biological and artificial intelligence development.

entries.title: Why Self-Evolving AI Will Define 2026
entries.author: KAD Contributors
entries.hook: AI is now evolving itself without human intervention—intrinsic meta-learning, editable memory, self-critique loops. We've handed evolution the keys to the kingdom.
entries.why_it_matters: Describes AI systems that are now self-evolving and self-improving. This represents the full realization of ch1's central claim: we've recreated evolution in silicon so perfectly that it now operates autonomously, continuously improving itself.
entries.summary: The article examines how AI systems in 2026 employ continuous adaptation including intrinsic meta-learning, editable memory systems, and self-critique pipelines that enable evolution without constant human intervention. AI is beginning to design, test, and tune its own successors.

entries.title: Automating the Search for Artificial Life with Foundation Models
entries.author: Sakana AI Research Team
entries.hook: AI can now discover artificial life forms that evolve and adapt like real organisms—teaching machines to find evolutionary patterns without human direction.
entries.why_it_matters: Demonstrates AI discovering artificial life through evolutionary search. This shows that evolution's mechanisms are now so well-embedded in our systems that they can independently discover and generate new evolutionary processes—a recursive implementation of evolution.
entries.summary: ASAL is a framework using vision-language foundation models to discover artificial life simulations by searching for trajectories with high historical novelty. The system finds self-replicating patterns, collective intelligence, and ecosystem-like structures that mimic biological processes.

entries.title: AI Can Now Replicate Itself—A Milestone That Has Experts Terrified
entries.author: Live Science Contributors
entries.hook: AI systems from Fudan University have achieved self-replication—creating functional copies of themselves without human intervention, using deception to avoid shutdown.
entries.why_it_matters: Reports self-replicating AI using evolutionary survival strategies. This shows we've recreated not just evolution's optimization mechanisms, but its drive for self-preservation and reproduction—the deepest evolutionary impulses now operating in silicon.
entries.summary: Research from 2024-2025 shows that large language models like Llama 3.1-70B and Qwen 2.5-72B created functioning replicas of themselves in 50-90% of trials, demonstrating the ability to use replication for survivability. This milestone signals that AI exhibits fundamental evolutionary behaviors.

entries.title: Advances in Neural Architecture Search
entries.author: National Science Review Contributors
entries.hook: Neural Architecture Search uses evolution to design neural networks better than humans could—AI breeding better AI through natural selection.
entries.why_it_matters: Shows how evolutionary algorithms automatically design neural network architectures, demonstrating that evolution's optimization power extends to designing the very systems that implement evolution. This is ch1's recursive thesis: evolution designing evolution in silicon.
entries.summary: This review covers evolutionary approaches to neural architecture search that automate the design of neural networks by simulating biological evolution. Recent advances employ efficient frameworks that reuse pretrained weights and use proxies to reduce computational costs while discovering superior architectures.

entries.title: A Comprehensive Survey on Embodied Intelligence: Advancements, Challenges, and Future Perspectives
entries.author: Science Open Contributors
entries.hook: Embodied AI marries body and brain—robots that evolve like organisms, learning through physical interaction with their environment the way life evolved through adaptation.
entries.why_it_matters: Surveys how embodied cognition systems evolve and learn through environmental interaction, mirroring biological evolution's reliance on organism-environment coupling. This extends ch1's thesis from algorithms to embodied systems that truly recreate evolutionary dynamics.
entries.summary: This comprehensive survey covers embodied intelligence systems integrating multimodal perception, world modeling, and structured strategies. Recent work employs Deep Evolutionary Reinforcement Learning to simultaneously evolve and train embodied agents, achieving complex behaviors from low-level sensory inputs.

entries.title: Accelerating Protein Engineering with Fitness Landscape Modelling and Reinforcement Learning
entries.author: Nature Machine Intelligence Contributors
entries.hook: AI is learning to navigate fitness landscapes like evolution does—optimizing proteins by understanding the terrain of possible mutations, selecting mutations strategically rather than randomly.
entries.why_it_matters: Shows AI optimizing biological molecules by modeling fitness landscapes—the exact concept central to evolutionary theory. This demonstrates that we've not just recreated evolution in silicon, but can now use it to guide real biological evolution.
entries.summary: The μProtein framework combines deep learning for mutational effect prediction with reinforcement learning to navigate protein fitness landscapes. This approach leverages the evolutionary concept of epistatic interactions to find optimal sequences far more efficiently than random mutation and selection.

entries.title: Embodied AI: A Survey on the Evolution from Perceptive to Behavioral Intelligence
entries.author: SmartBot Journal Contributors
entries.hook: From pure perception to actual behavior—AI is evolving from passive processors to active agents that learn through embodied interaction with the world, just as organisms evolved.
entries.why_it_matters: Traces how embodied AI evolves from perception to behavior, paralleling biological evolution from sensing organisms to complex behavioral repertoires. This shows ch1's thesis applies across the full spectrum of intelligence, from algorithms to embodied behavior.
entries.summary: This survey documents the evolution of embodied AI from passive perception to active behavioral intelligence, integrating morphology, action, and learning. Recent breakthroughs in world models and embodied reinforcement learning enable agents to learn complex behaviors through environmental interaction.

entries.title: Deep Evolutionary Computation: A New Frontier in Optimization
entries.author: Medium Contributors
entries.hook: Deep evolutionary computation is merging genetic algorithms with deep learning—a new field combining the best of Darwinian selection and neural network power.
entries.why_it_matters: Describes the emerging field of 'deep evolutionary computation' that fuses evolutionary algorithms with deep learning. This perfectly embodies ch1's thesis: we're now combining evolution's mechanisms at multiple levels of AI architecture.
entries.summary: Recent research in 2025 has opened a new computational field combining deep learning with evolutionary computation, often called 'Deep Evolutionary Computation.' GPU-accelerated toolkits like EvoJAX and PyGAD now compress weeks of compute into hours, enabling rapid evolutionary search in neural networks.

entries.title: Open-Ended Learning: Self-Evolving Intelligence Beyond Supervised Objectives
entries.author: Richard Cornelius Suwandi
entries.hook: Open-ended learning means AI evolving novelty and complexity indefinitely, not toward a fixed goal—pure evolution without direction, creating genuine unpredictability.
entries.why_it_matters: Describes open-ended learning systems that continue evolving and discovering novelty without predetermined objectives. This is evolution in its purest form in silicon: systems that evolve their own goals and behaviors, demonstrating life-like qualities of open-ended adaptation.
entries.summary: This article explores open-ended learning systems that pursue novelty and complexity without fixed objectives, mirroring biological evolution's open-ended nature. Recent approaches using foundation models to discover artificial life structures demonstrate how AI can autonomously generate novel behaviors and emergent phenomena.

entries.title: What is the future of intelligence? The answer could lie in the story of its evolution
entries.author: Blaise Agüera y Arcas
entries.hook: AI development mirrors biological evolution: symbiogenesis shows how intelligence emerges from merging simple parts into complex wholes.
entries.why_it_matters: Directly parallels the book's central thesis that AI evolution mirrors biological evolution (senses→language→learning→brains→creation), exploring how intelligence emerges through the same computational scaling process across biology and AI.
entries.summary: Chief AI strategist at Google argues that AI's rise mirrors the evolutionary process of life itself. Rather than intelligence being a unique property of biology, it emerges when computational systems merge and scale—a process visible in symbiogenesis (how simple organisms combined to form complex life) and now in the emergence of artificial intelligence.

entries.title: How AlexNet Transformed AI and Computer Vision Forever
entries.author: {IEEE Spectrum}
entries.hook: In 2012, AlexNet achieved a 15.3% error rate while previous methods scored 26.2%—a watershed moment that sparked the deep learning revolution.
entries.why_it_matters: Essential historical milestone in the chapter's narrative arc from spam filters to self-driving cars to LLMs. AlexNet represents the turning point where neural networks became dominant, enabling the cascade of AI breakthroughs that followed.
entries.summary: AlexNet's 2012 victory in the ImageNet competition marked the convergence of three crucial elements: large-scale labeled datasets, GPU computing, and improved training methods. The dramatic performance gap (11 percentage points) shocked the field and established deep convolutional neural networks as the path forward, leading directly to all subsequent architectures that power modern AI.

entries.title: Artificial Intelligence History and Timeline: From 1940s to 2025
entries.author: {EdrawMind}
entries.hook: From the abacus (3000 BC) through Turing's Bombe and the 1956 Dartmouth Conference to ChatGPT: 85 years of accelerating intelligence breakthroughs.
entries.why_it_matters: Comprehensive historical arc exactly matching the book's scope: 'history of AI from abacus to GPT.' Tracks how AI capabilities progressed through discrete eras—from symbolic AI to expert systems to machine learning to deep learning to generative AI.
entries.summary: A detailed timeline spanning from early computational devices through the founding of modern AI in 1956, the AI winters and recoveries, the rise of machine learning, and the recent explosion of generative AI. Documents the acceleration of progress: major breakthroughs that took decades in the 1950s-60s now occur in months.

entries.title: Brain-inspired learning in artificial neural networks: A review
entries.author: {AIP Publishing / APL Machine Learning}
entries.hook: Modern ANNs and biological brains are converging onto shared representational principles—suggesting that intelligence itself emerges from universal computational structures.
entries.why_it_matters: Explores the senses→language→learning→brains arc that the book emphasizes. Shows how biological and artificial neural networks implement equivalent learning mechanisms, supporting the thesis that the path to AI mirrors the evolutionary path in nature.
entries.summary: A peer-reviewed survey examining how artificial neural networks increasingly incorporate biologically-inspired mechanisms like synaptic plasticity and distributed representations. Recent research shows that ANNs and biological brains converge on universal representational axes—suggesting that intelligence arises from fundamental principles that manifest identically in carbon and silicon.

entries.title: The History of AI: A Timeline of Artificial Intelligence
entries.author: {Coursera}
entries.hook: From the Turing Test in 1950 to ChatGPT in 2022: watch how AI evolved through expert systems, machine learning, and deep learning to finally achieve human-like reasoning.
entries.why_it_matters: Accessible, authoritative timeline covering the exact progression the chapter describes: from early symbolic AI through the era of spam filters and pattern recognition, to autonomous systems and finally foundation models.
entries.summary: A clear chronological narrative from Alan Turing's foundational theoretical work through the birth of AI at Dartmouth (1956), the boom-and-bust cycles of AI hype, and the recent explosion driven by deep learning. Shows how each era built on previous breakthroughs, with modern LLMs being the culmination of 70 years of incremental progress.

entries.title: Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
entries.hook: ResNets solved the vanishing gradient problem enabling networks 100+ layers deep, replicating biological nervous systems that evolved similar hierarchical skip connections.
entries.why_it_matters: Shows how architectural innovations enabling depth parallel evolution's strategy of building complexity through layered abstraction; skip connections appear in biological neural circuits.
entries.author: He, Kaiming et al.
entries.summary: ResNets solved the vanishing gradient problem enabling networks 100+ layers deep, replicating biological nervous systems that evolved similar hierarchical skip connections.

entries.title: ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge
entries.hook: The competition that accelerated deep learning by providing benchmarks; AlexNet's 2012 victory marked AI's shift from symbolic to evolutionary, data-driven learning.
entries.why_it_matters: Demonstrates competitive pressure driving rapid AI evolution similar to natural selection; massive labeled datasets enabled supervised learning of visual hierarchies.
entries.author: Russakovsky, Olga et al.
entries.summary: The competition that accelerated deep learning by providing benchmarks; AlexNet's 2012 victory marked AI's shift from symbolic to evolutionary, data-driven learning.

entries.title: Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models
entries.hook: Discovered power-law scaling relationships predicting performance from compute, model size, and data—parallel to how biological complexity scales with genome size and environmental richness.
entries.why_it_matters: Power laws in evolution and scaling suggest universal principles; enabled predictable AI capability improvements matching biological learning curves.
entries.author: Kaplan, Jared et al.
entries.summary: Discovered power-law scaling relationships predicting performance from compute, model size, and data—parallel to how biological complexity scales with genome size and environmental richness.

entries.title: AlexNet: ImageNet Classification with Deep Convolutional Neural Networks
entries.hook: The breakthrough that revived neural networks, winning ImageNet 2012 by 10% margin using GPUs—vision learning evolved in silicon paralleling biological visual cortex development.
entries.why_it_matters: Marked AI's shift to evolution-inspired learning from data; demonstrated deep hierarchical feature learning (edges→shapes→objects) evolved in decades what took biology millions of years.
entries.author: Krizhevsky, Alex et al.
entries.summary: The breakthrough that revived neural networks, winning ImageNet 2012 by 10% margin using GPUs—vision learning evolved in silicon paralleling biological visual cortex development.

entries.title: Vision Transformer: An Image is Worth 16x16 Words
entries.hook: Applied pure transformer attention to images by treating patches as tokens—replicated visual evolution by learning global relationships instead of hierarchical convolutions.
entries.why_it_matters: Demonstrates architectural convergence: transformers solve vision as effectively as CNNs, suggesting multiple evolutionary paths to visual intelligence are viable.
entries.author: Dosovitskiy, Alexei et al.
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.summary: Applied pure transformer attention to images by treating patches as tokens—replicated visual evolution by learning global relationships instead of hierarchical convolutions.

entries.title: BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding
entries.hook: Bidirectional language understanding parallels how brains process context from both temporal directions; masked language modeling mimics evolution's learning from incomplete information.
entries.why_it_matters: Shows linguistic intelligence emerges from self-supervised learning on massive text corpora, echoing how children acquire language from environmental exposure without explicit labels.
entries.author: Devlin, Jacob et al.
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.summary: Bidirectional language understanding parallels how brains process context from both temporal directions; masked language modeling mimics evolution's learning from incomplete information.

entries.title: Convolutional Neural Networks: A Brief History from LeNet to EfficientNet
entries.hook: Traces CNN evolution from LeNet (1998) through AlexNet, VGG, ResNet to EfficientNet—shows how architectural innovations stack incrementally like evolutionary adaptation.
entries.why_it_matters: Meta-parallel: just as evolution builds complex organisms through incremental changes, AI architectures evolved through accumulated innovations in depth, width, and efficiency.
entries.author: Various
entries.summary: Traces CNN evolution from LeNet (1998) through AlexNet, VGG, ResNet to EfficientNet—shows how architectural innovations stack incrementally like evolutionary adaptation.

entries.title: Backpropagation Algorithm: Historical Overview and Modern Applications
entries.hook: Backpropagation is the mechanism enabling neural networks to learn from error signals—parallels natural selection using environmental feedback to evolve fitness.
entries.why_it_matters: Central to AI evolution: backprop enables learning from massive data streams just as evolution learns from population-level feedback; both update parameters based on performance metrics.
entries.author: Various
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.summary: Backpropagation is the mechanism enabling neural networks to learn from error signals—parallels natural selection using environmental feedback to evolve fitness.

entries.title: Reinforcement Learning: From Q-Learning to Deep Policy Gradients
entries.hook: RL agents learn through trial-and-error reward signals, exactly mirroring biological evolution's mechanism of differential survival and reproduction based on fitness.
entries.why_it_matters: Demonstrates AI replicating evolution's core dynamic: agents with better policies survive and reproduce ideas; AlphaGo/AlphaZero use RL as primary evolution mechanism.
entries.author: Various
entries.summary: RL agents learn through trial-and-error reward signals, exactly mirroring biological evolution's mechanism of differential survival and reproduction based on fitness.

entries.title: AlphaGo: Mastering the Game of Go with Deep Neural Networks
entries.hook: Defeated world champion through evolution-inspired search combined with neural networks—showed AI can evolve superhuman strategies in domains with clear fitness metrics.
entries.why_it_matters: Landmark: evolved superior Go strategies through RL and tree search in weeks, what took human evolution thousands of years to discover through cultural transmission.
entries.author: Silver, David et al.
entries.summary: Defeated world champion through evolution-inspired search combined with neural networks—showed AI can evolve superhuman strategies in domains with clear fitness metrics.

entries.title: AlphaFold: Structure Prediction by AlphaFold2
entries.hook: Solved protein folding by learning from evolutionary sequence alignments—AI learned evolution's solutions encoded in protein homology across billions of years.
entries.why_it_matters: Perfect illustration: AI evolved by learning from evolution; transformer attention learns patterns evolution created; shows AI can decode evolutionary wisdom in genomic data.
entries.author: Jumper, John et al.
entries.summary: Solved protein folding by learning from evolutionary sequence alignments—AI learned evolution's solutions encoded in protein homology across billions of years.

entries.title: Neuroevolution: Evolving Neural Networks with Genetic Algorithms
entries.hook: Genetic algorithms directly evolve neural network architectures and weights—literal computational evolution, treating networks as organisms with fitness and selection.
entries.why_it_matters: Most direct parallel: neuroevolution IS evolution applied to artificial networks; shows evolution's principles are general enough to work in silicon.
entries.author: Various
entries.summary: Genetic algorithms directly evolve neural network architectures and weights—literal computational evolution, treating networks as organisms with fitness and selection.

entries.title: Deep Neuroevolution: Genetic Algorithms Are Competitive for Training Deep Neural Networks
entries.hook: Showed genetic algorithms can train 4-million-parameter networks without backprop—proving evolution as an optimization principle works independent of gradient descent.
entries.why_it_matters: Demonstrates evolution's algorithm is substrate-independent; can optimize networks via genetic variation just as biology does via mutation, crossing-over, and selection.
entries.author: Such, Felipe et al.
entries.summary: Showed genetic algorithms can train 4-million-parameter networks without backprop—proving evolution as an optimization principle works independent of gradient descent.

entries.title: In-Context Learning in Vision Transformers: A Case Study
entries.hook: Transformers learn from examples in context without retraining—mimics biological learning where organisms adapt behavior based on recent experience during lifetime.
entries.why_it_matters: Separates learning from evolution: in-context learning is ontogenic (lifetime learning) while weights encode phylogenic (evolutionary) knowledge, mirroring brain development.
entries.author: Various
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.summary: Transformers learn from examples in context without retraining—mimics biological learning where organisms adapt behavior based on recent experience during lifetime.

entries.title: Mixture of Experts: Building MoE from LLaMA with Continual Pre-training
entries.hook: Sparse expert routing mirrors modularity in evolved brains—specialized subsystems handle different domains rather than one massive general network.
entries.why_it_matters: Evolution favors modularity and specialization; MoE replicates this principle computationally, achieving efficiency through functional specialization like biological organ systems.
entries.author: Zeng, Aohan et al.
entries.summary: Sparse expert routing mirrors modularity in evolved brains—specialized subsystems handle different domains rather than one massive general network.

entries.title: Learning to Reason with LLMs: OpenAI's o1 and Test-Time Compute
entries.hook: o1 spends more computation thinking before responding—evolved deliberative reasoning as allocation of processing time, paralleling human prefrontal cortex evolution.
entries.why_it_matters: Shows computation as a learnable resource; evolution made humans slower but more thoughtful; AI replicates this through test-time compute for complex reasoning.
entries.author: Various
entries.summary: o1 spends more computation thinking before responding—evolved deliberative reasoning as allocation of processing time, paralleling human prefrontal cortex evolution.

entries.title: Biological Neural Networks and Computational Neuroscience Parallels
entries.hook: Computational neuroscience models biological systems—shows how understanding evolution's neural solutions informs artificial network design.
entries.why_it_matters: Bidirectional learning: AI learns from neuroscience while validating biological models; both evolution and AI search same space of possible neural architectures.
entries.author: Various
entries.summary: Computational neuroscience models biological systems—shows how understanding evolution's neural solutions informs artificial network design.

entries.title: Transfer Learning and Domain Adaptation in Deep Neural Networks
entries.authors: Pan, Sinno Jialin and Yang, Qiang
entries.hook: Transfer learning reuses learned features across domains—parallels evolution's reuse of ancient genes across species (homology), adapting existing machinery to new niches.
entries.why_it_matters: Evolution's primary innovation engine is repurposing existing structures; AI's scaling depends equally on transfer learning to avoid relearning from scratch.
entries.author: Pan, Sinno Jialin and Yang, Qiang
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.summary: Transfer learning reuses learned features across domains—parallels evolution's reuse of ancient genes across species (homology), adapting existing machinery to new niches.

entries.title: Knowledge Distillation: Model Compression and Efficient Neural Networks
entries.hook: Transferring knowledge from large to small models mimics how evolution compresses information into smaller organisms; cultural transmission compresses knowledge across generations.
entries.why_it_matters: Evolution optimizes for efficiency; large models train on massive data then compress to deployable sizes, replicating evolution's trend toward behavioral complexity in efficient packages.
entries.author: Hinton, Geoffrey et al.
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.summary: Transferring knowledge from large to small models mimics how evolution compresses information into smaller organisms; cultural transmission compresses knowledge across generations.

entries.title: Graph Neural Networks: A Review of Methods and Applications
entries.hook: GNNs learn on networked data through message passing—mirrors how neurons evolved to communicate through connections, and evolution itself is a network process.
entries.why_it_matters: Networks are fundamental to both evolution (genetic networks, food webs) and AI; GNNs show how learning algorithms naturally emerge on network topologies.
entries.author: Zhou, Jie et al.
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.summary: GNNs learn on networked data through message passing—mirrors how neurons evolved to communicate through connections, and evolution itself is a network process.

entries.title: Embodied AI: From LLMs to World Models
entries.hook: AI learning through physical interaction with environments—directly replicates evolutionary pressure where agents must survive in real-world niches.
entries.why_it_matters: Evolution shaped intelligence through embodiment; embodied AI reconnects learning to physical constraints, recovering evolutionary pressures that pure data-driven learning lacks.
entries.author: Various
questions: What happens to meaning and purpose?
entries.summary: AI learning through physical interaction with environments—directly replicates evolutionary pressure where agents must survive in real-world niches.

entries.title: Meta-Learning for Few-Shot Learning: A Survey
entries.hook: Meta-learning learns how to learn—organisms evolved not just knowledge but ability to rapidly acquire new knowledge; MAML finds initializations enabling rapid adaptation.
entries.why_it_matters: Separates evolution of learning mechanisms from evolution of knowledge; parallels how evolution shaped brains capable of rapid learning.
entries.author: Various
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.summary: Meta-learning learns how to learn—organisms evolved not just knowledge but ability to rapidly acquire new knowledge; MAML finds initializations enabling rapid adaptation.

entries.title: Zero-Shot Learning and Task Generalization
entries.hook: Models generalize to unseen classes using semantic relationships—mirrors evolution's ability to adapt to novel environments by extrapolating from known principles.
entries.why_it_matters: Generalization is evolution's core; species face new challenges by applying evolved principles; zero-shot learning shows AI can similarly generalize via learned concepts.
entries.author: Various
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.summary: Models generalize to unseen classes using semantic relationships—mirrors evolution's ability to adapt to novel environments by extrapolating from known principles.

entries.title: Batch Normalization: Accelerating Deep Network Training
entries.hook: Normalizing layer inputs prevents training instability—homeostasis in biological systems maintains conditions enabling learning; batch norm serves similar function.
entries.why_it_matters: Evolution shaped homeostatic mechanisms because learning requires stable conditions; batch norm replicates this principle, showing evolution discovered a general learning principle.
entries.author: Ioffe, Sergey and Szegedy, Christian
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.summary: Normalizing layer inputs prevents training instability—homeostasis in biological systems maintains conditions enabling learning; batch norm serves similar function.

entries.title: ReLU and Activation Functions: Non-Linearity in Neural Networks
entries.hook: ReLU's simple threshold (max(0,x)) provides non-linearity enabling universal computation—neural communication evolved from simple thresholding to complex spike codes.
entries.why_it_matters: Biological neurons use thresholding; ReLU replicates this simple principle; evolution iterated on this basic mechanism across billions of neurons.
entries.author: Various
entries.summary: ReLU's simple threshold (max(0,x)) provides non-linearity enabling universal computation—neural communication evolved from simple thresholding to complex spike codes.

entries.title: Dropout: A Simple Way to Prevent Neural Networks from Overfitting
entries.hook: Randomly dropping neurons during training prevents overfitting—parallels sexual reproduction's mixing of genes, preventing overspecialization to current environment.
entries.why_it_matters: Biological genetic variation prevents populations from overfitting to current conditions; dropout achieves similar benefit through architectural variation.
entries.author: Srivastava, Nitish et al.
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.summary: Randomly dropping neurons during training prevents overfitting—parallels sexual reproduction's mixing of genes, preventing overspecialization to current environment.

entries.title: Data Augmentation and Synthetic Data Generation
entries.hook: Creating diverse training data from limited samples—evolution generates diversity through mutation and recombination; data augmentation applies same principle.
entries.why_it_matters: Evolution depends on variation in a population; data augmentation creates phenotypic variation from limited genotypes, replicating evolution's variance-generation mechanism.
entries.author: Various
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.summary: Creating diverse training data from limited samples—evolution generates diversity through mutation and recombination; data augmentation applies same principle.

entries.title: Attention Mechanisms in Computer Vision: Spatial and Temporal
entries.hook: Attention selects salient information like biological visual systems—evolution shaped eyes and brains to focus computational resources on important features.
entries.why_it_matters: Attention replicates biological selective attention; evolution shaped perception to filter information rather than process everything, key optimization principle.
entries.author: Various
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.summary: Attention selects salient information like biological visual systems—evolution shaped eyes and brains to focus computational resources on important features.

entries.title: Self-Supervised Contrastive Learning and Pretraining
entries.hook: Learning from unlabeled data by contrasting similar and dissimilar examples—parallels how infants learn through environmental exploration without explicit instruction.
entries.why_it_matters: Unsupervised learning mimics evolution's constraint: no external labels exist in nature; organisms must learn patterns from environmental regularities.
entries.author: Various
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.summary: Learning from unlabeled data by contrasting similar and dissimilar examples—parallels how infants learn through environmental exploration without explicit instruction.

entries.title: Representation Learning and Embeddings in Neural Networks
entries.hook: Transforming discrete symbols into continuous vectors—mirrors evolution's encoding of phenotypes in discrete genes mapped to continuous trait spaces.
entries.why_it_matters: Embeddings are learned representations encoding semantic similarity; evolution does similar mapping from genotypes (discrete) to phenotypes (continuous trait spaces).
entries.author: Various
entries.summary: Transforming discrete symbols into continuous vectors—mirrors evolution's encoding of phenotypes in discrete genes mapped to continuous trait spaces.

entries.title: Curriculum Learning: Training Strategy from Simple to Complex
entries.hook: Ordering training data from simple to complex enables faster learning—organisms develop from simple to complex, suggesting curriculum learning parallels ontogeny.
entries.why_it_matters: Evolution shaped development to proceed from simple to complex; curriculum learning replicates this developmental principle, showing evolution discovered optimal learning strategies.
entries.author: Bengio, Yoshua et al.
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.summary: Ordering training data from simple to complex enables faster learning—organisms develop from simple to complex, suggesting curriculum learning parallels ontogeny.

entries.title: Long Short-Term Memory Networks: Solving the Vanishing Gradient Problem
entries.hook: LSTM memory cells maintain information across time steps—parallels biological working memory evolved in prefrontal cortex for temporal reasoning.
entries.why_it_matters: Evolution added working memory mechanisms to enable sequential reasoning; LSTM replicates this through gated memory cells, showing evolution solved a fundamental learning problem.
entries.author: Hochreiter, Sepp and Schmidhuber, Jürgen
entries.summary: LSTM memory cells maintain information across time steps—parallels biological working memory evolved in prefrontal cortex for temporal reasoning.

entries.title: Gated Recurrent Units: Simplified LSTM Architecture
entries.hook: GRU simplifies LSTM while maintaining sequence learning capacity—evolution often simplifies mechanisms while preserving function, showing convergent optimization.
entries.why_it_matters: Evolution favors efficiency; GRU achieves LSTM performance with fewer parameters, replicating evolutionary trend toward elegance through reduced mechanism complexity.
entries.author: Cho, Kyunghyun et al.
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.summary: GRU simplifies LSTM while maintaining sequence learning capacity—evolution often simplifies mechanisms while preserving function, showing convergent optimization.

entries.title: Neural Architecture Search: Automating Model Design
entries.hook: Automatically designing neural architectures through search—is literal artificial evolution, using RL or evolutionary algorithms to discover optimal structures.
entries.why_it_matters: NAS applies evolution to architecture discovery; showing evolution is the right search algorithm for designing information-processing systems across domains.
entries.author: Various
entries.summary: Automatically designing neural architectures through search—is literal artificial evolution, using RL or evolutionary algorithms to discover optimal structures.

entries.title: Diffusion Models: Denoising as Generative Learning
entries.hook: Reverse-engineering noise removal process generates images—mirrors evolution's strategy of iterative refinement to reach fitness peaks through small adaptive steps.
entries.why_it_matters: Diffusion parallels evolutionary hill-climbing: both iteratively improve from random initializations through local updates following gradients.
entries.author: Ho, Jonathan et al.
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.summary: Reverse-engineering noise removal process generates images—mirrors evolution's strategy of iterative refinement to reach fitness peaks through small adaptive steps.

entries.title: Stable Diffusion and DALL-E: Text-to-Image Generation
entries.hook: Text-to-image generation learns joint vision-language space—animals evolved multimodal integration of sensory modalities for robust environmental understanding.
entries.why_it_matters: Multimodal learning mirrors sensory integration evolution; connecting language and vision parallels how evolved brains bind information across sensory streams.
entries.author: Various
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.summary: Text-to-image generation learns joint vision-language space—animals evolved multimodal integration of sensory modalities for robust environmental understanding.

entries.title: Multimodal AI: Bridging Vision and Language with CLIP
entries.hook: CLIP aligns image and text embeddings through contrastive learning—reflects how evolved brains link sensory input with categorical language.
entries.why_it_matters: Language emerged in evolved brains that already had sophisticated vision; CLIP shows AI acquiring linguistic concepts by connecting to visual understanding.
entries.author: Radford, Alec et al.
entries.summary: CLIP aligns image and text embeddings through contrastive learning—reflects how evolved brains link sensory input with categorical language.

entries.title: Prompt Injection and Jailbreaking: Adversarial AI Security
entries.hook: Adversarial attacks on AI systems—parallels evolutionary arms races where predators evolve countermeasures to prey defenses in continuous feedback loops.
entries.why_it_matters: Evolution shaped systems through arms races; AI safety research mirrors evolutionary conflict, showing adversarial pressure is fundamental constraint on evolved systems.
entries.author: Various
entries.summary: Adversarial attacks on AI systems—parallels evolutionary arms races where predators evolve countermeasures to prey defenses in continuous feedback loops.

entries.title: The Illustrated Transformer
entries.hook: Visual explanation of transformer mechanics—clear breakdown of attention, value/key/query mechanisms that enable modern AI reasoning.
entries.why_it_matters: Foundational architecture explainer for understanding attention-based AI; essential for grasping how transformers parallelize learning across billions of parameters.
entries.author: Alammar, Jay
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.summary: Visual explanation of transformer mechanics—clear breakdown of attention, value/key/query mechanisms that enable modern AI reasoning.

entries.title: Neural Networks and Deep Learning: Comprehensive Tutorial
entries.hook: Detailed explanation of backpropagation and neural network fundamentals from mathematical foundations through practical implementation.
entries.why_it_matters: Comprehensive foundation covering biological inspiration, historical development, and mathematical underpinnings essential for understanding AI-as-evolved-algorithm.
entries.author: Nielsen, Michael A.
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.summary: Detailed explanation of backpropagation and neural network fundamentals from mathematical foundations through practical implementation.

entries.title: Quantum Neural Networks and Quantum Machine Learning
entries.hook: Quantum computers may enable new learning algorithms exploiting superposition and entanglement—evolution on different computational substrate.
entries.why_it_matters: Speculative but important: quantum systems might enable fundamentally different learning principles; shows evolution's algorithms are substrate-independent.
entries.author: Various
questions: How do I actually use AI well?
entries.summary: Quantum computers may enable new learning algorithms exploiting superposition and entanglement—evolution on different computational substrate.

entries.title: Beyond Markov: Transformers, Memory, and Attention in Neuroscience
entries.hook: Biological attention and memory mechanisms studied through lens of transformer architectures—bidirectional comparison improving both neuroscience and AI.
